# crimes — codebase risk workflow

`crimes` is a deterministic CLI (no LLM) that reports change risk and
agent risk. JSON output is the stable contract; prefer it when planning.

## When to run it

- Before editing an unfamiliar file: `crimes context <file> --format json`
- Before a broad refactor: `crimes scan <path> --format json`
- After edits: `crimes scan --changed --format json`
- Before merging a branch: `crimes verdict --format json`

## Decision rules

- Treat any new `severity: "high"` finding introduced by your edit as a blocker unless the user explicitly accepts it.
- Read `evidence[]` before acting; it contains deterministic facts, not LLM opinion.
- Use `scores.agent_risk` to decide which findings need human attention first.
- If a finding is a false positive, record feedback with `crimes feedback <fingerprint> --verdict fp --note "<why>"` rather than silently ignoring it.

/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.2-r3/claude/review-01-messy-ts-app.json:7:  "response": "# PR review: `01-messy-ts-app` baseline introduction\n\n**Headline:** 1 hard block + 2 escalated to block by interaction. Refund flow is the standout — three findings (`crime_00008`, `crime_00011`, plus the time-dependent invoice logic) compose into a single agent footgun that's worse than any of them alone.\n\n**Decision threshold I'm using:**\n- **Block** — high severity, OR mediums whose `agent_risk ≥ 0.5` *and* sit on a correctness-critical path (billing, refunds, money), OR mediums that compose with other findings to mask business rules\n- **Warn** — must address but not before merge; medium severity OR low severity that actively misleads\n- **Ignore** — fix opportunistically; low impact, cosmetic, or already covered by a bigger finding\n\n---\n\n## Block merge (3)\n\n| ID | Charge | Sev / agent_risk | Why it blocks |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| `crime_00001` | God Function — `generateInvoice` 204 lines | **0.9 / 0.63** | Only `high` in the report. 3.4× threshold inside `billing.ts`. Anything else we add to invoicing lands on top of a function nobody can hold in their head. Refactor before further billing work, not after. |\n| `crime_00008` | False Identity — `calculateRefundPreview` sends email + writes audit | 0.55 / 0.52 | Sub-block scores in isolation, but a name that promises \"preview\" while issuing refund-side-effects is exactly the agent-risk failure mode `crimes` is built for. An agent told \"just compute the preview\" will fire emails. Block on the *kind* of risk, not just the number. |\n| `crime_00011` | Logic in the Alibi — refund authorisation rule lives in a comment | 0.5 / 0.5 | \"Only owners can refund enterprise plans unless billing support approves\" — that's a regulated business rule sitting in prose four lines above the function flagged in `crime_00008`. Comment-rule + misleading-name + temporal coupling (`crime_00004`) at the same file is a refund flow nobody should merge as-is. |\n\nThe three above are tightly coupled (all in `src/billing.ts` lines 39–57). I'd ask the PR author to address them as one unit.\n\n---\n\n## Warn (12)\n\nMust fix, but not blocking merge. Grouped by theme.\n\n**Agent context & domain literals**\n- `crime_00002` — Missing Agent Context (0.6 / 0.61). Repo ships no `AGENTS.md` / `CLAUDE.md`. High `agent_risk` for a structural finding. Warn rather than block because it's repo-wide hygiene, not a defect in this PR's code.\n- `crime_00007` — String Sprinkles `\"enterprise\"` in 4 files / 4 areas (0.5 / 0.6). One more copy and it's de-facto duplicated policy. Centralise before adding another plan tier.\n- `crime_00009` — Concept Alias Drift, tenant/account/org/team/workspace (0.55 / 0.58). Five terms for one concept across docs and routes. Pick one.\n\n**Time-dependent billing (correctness-adjacent)**\n- `crime_00004` — Temporal Recklessness in `src/billing.ts` × 7 calls (0.6 / 0.53). Invoice generation reading wall time means tests can't pin a moment. Should be block-grade in a vacuum, but is partly subsumed by `crime_00001` (any extract pass will inject a clock).\n- `crime_00005` — Temporal Recklessness in `src/date.ts` × 5 calls (0.6 / 0.61). Central date utility, `test_gap` 1.0. Fix alongside `crime_00004`.\n\n**Navigation / routing drift**\n- `crime_00012` — Duplicated Navigation Source, `/settings/billing` labeled \"Plans\" vs \"Billing\" (0.6 / 0.57).\n- `crime_00014` — Route Metadata Drift, `/settings/billing` labeled four different ways across path/file/component/title/nav (0.6 / 0.56). Picks up the same destination as `crime_00012`.\n- `crime_00021/22/23` — three Orphaned Destinations: `/account/subscription`, `/team`, `/workspace/members` (0.4 / 0.46 each). Three abandoned routes in one PR is a smell worth raising as a single warn — feature was likely half-extracted. Either link them or delete them.\n\n**Structure & process**\n- `crime_00010` — God File `billing.ts` 310 lines (0.6 / 0.5). Partly resolved by addressing `crime_00001`; warn rather than double-block.\n- `crime_00006` — Test That Proves Nothing, \"renders the enterprise plan\" — zero assertions (0.45 / 0.46). Misleading test signal; delete or assert. Pair with `crime_00016`.\n- `crime_00013` — Unfinished Business in `src/todo.ts`, 14 TODO/FIXME at 424/1k LOC (0.4 / 0.57). The density itself is the signal — agents can't tell load-bearing from aspirational.\n\n---\n\n## Ignore (7)\n\nLow impact, fix opportunistically, or already covered by a higher finding.\n\n| ID | Charge | Sev / agent_risk | Reason to ignore |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| `crime_00015` | Docs-Code Drift, `docs/teams.md → ./setup.md` missing | 0.35 / 0.51 | Single dead link in a doc. Trivial fix. |\n| `crime_00016` | Test That Proves Nothing, \"creates an invoice\" weak matcher | 0.25 / 0.34 | Same category as `crime_00006`; fix as part of that warn. |\n| `crime_00017` | Option Bag Junk Drawer, `describeCheckout` | 0.3 / 0.48 | Petty crime — name the shape when it next changes. |\n| `crime_00018` | Return Shape Roulette, `resolvePaymentResult` 3 shapes | 0.3 / 0.48 | Annoying but contained to `petty.ts`. |\n| `crime_00019` | Negative Flag Maze, `!disableBilling && !skipRetry` | 0.25 / 0.47 | Cosmetic; rename when the condition next grows. |\n| `crime_00020` | Unfinished Business in `billing.ts`, 12.9/1k LOC | 0.2 / 0.4 | Just over threshold. Resolve naturally during `crime_00001` refactor. |\n\n---\n\n## Cross-finding patterns worth flagging on the PR\n\n1. **Refund cluster** (`crime_00001` + `crime_00008` + `crime_00011` + `crime_00004` lines 50–57): four findings in 220 lines, all on the refund/invoice path. Treat as one refactor.\n2. **`/settings/billing` identity crisis** (`crime_00012` + `crime_00014`): same destination, two findings, four labels — fix together.\n3. **Half-finished tenant feature** (`crime_00009` + `crime_00021–23`): three orphan routes (`/account`, `/team`, `/workspace`) plus the five-word concept drift across those exact files suggests a partially abandoned tenancy rename. The PR author should confirm intent before merge.\n\n**Bottom line:** I'd block on the refund cluster (1, 8, 11), accept the warn list as PR comments, and let the ignore list ride.",

Centralizing into a constant/enum is a small change that prevents the next agent from adding a fifth copy with subtly different policy.\n\n**5. `crime_00012` — Duplicated Navigation Source (`/settings/billing`)** → **Fix now**\nSame destination, two labels (\"Plans\" vs \"Billing\") across `registry.ts` and `sidebar.ts`. /Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.2-r2/claude/plan-06-stress-duplication.json:7:  "response": "## Refactor plan: consolidate sanitise + role checks\n\nTwo ordered steps. Policy first because the sanitise helpers contain the role check — unifying the predicate first means step 2 collapses to a single helper with a clean dependency, instead of inlining `role === \"admin\"` into a \"shared\" helper that still bakes in the literal.\n\n### Step 1 — Extract the `\"admin\"` policy predicate (crime_00003)\n\n**Scope:** 7 call sites across 4 files (`policy.ts` ×4, `sanitize-a.ts:7`, plus the +2 in `sanitize-b.ts` / `sanitize-c.ts`).\n\n**Change:**\n- In `src/policy.ts`, add `export function isAdmin(role: string): boolean { return role === \"admin\"; }`.\n- Replace every `role === \"admin\"` with `isAdmin(role)`. In `policy.ts` this also dedupes the 4 internal checks against itself — they all call the new function.\n- Import `isAdmin` into `sanitize-a.ts`, `sanitize-b.ts`, `sanitize-c.ts` ahead of step 2 (the import will survive the next step).\n\n**Why this is safe to ship on its own:** pure rename of a boolean expression. No behaviour change, no new module surface area beyond one exported function. Tests that pass today will pass after.\n\n**Verification:** `rg 'role\\s*===\\s*\"admin\"'` should return zero hits after this step. `crime_00003` should drop out of the next `crimes scan`.\n\n### Step 2 — Extract the shared sanitise helper (crime_00002)\n\n**Scope:** the three near-duplicate functions — `sanitizeUserInput` (`sanitize-a.ts:10-36`), `sanitizeText` (`sanitize-b.ts:9-35`), `cleanMessage` (`sanitize-c.ts:9-35`).\n\n**Change:**\n- Create `src/sanitize.ts` exporting a single `sanitize(input: string, role: string): string` whose body is the consolidated version of the three near-duplicates (reconcile the small per-copy drifts deliberately — pick the strictest rule per branch and note the choice in the commit message; this is where duplication has been hiding silent divergence).\n- Internally, the new helper calls `isAdmin(role)` from step 1 instead of comparing the literal.\n- In `sanitize-a/b/c.ts`, replace the function bodies with one-line delegations to `sanitize(...)`, keeping the existing exported names (`sanitizeUserInput`, `sanitizeText`, `cleanMessage`) so callers don't move. This keeps the diff to one file's worth of new logic plus three trivial re-exports.\n\n**Why this order matters:** if step 2 ran first, the consolidated helper would either (a) still contain `role === \"admin\"` — leaving `crime_00003` unresolved and now centralised in a misleading place — or (b) be edited twice. Doing policy first means step 2's diff is purely \"merge three near-duplicates,\" which is the change that actually needs review.\n\n**Verification:** `crime_00002` should drop out. The three wrapper exports can be removed in a follow-up once callers are migrated to `sanitize` directly — explicitly out of scope for this change set to keep the blast radius small.\n\n### Out of scope (flagged, not addressed)\n\n- `crime_00001` (exact duplicate `recordSanitiseEvent` in `telemetry-a/b.ts`) — same pattern, but unrelated to sanitise/role and worth a separate commit.\n- `crime_00004` / `crime_00005` (`Date.now()` in the telemetry files) — handle together with crime_00001 via clock injection in the telemetry helper, not here.",

Decompose `generateInvoice` (crime_00001, reinforced by crime_00010)\nThe 204-line `generateInvoice` (lines 50–253) is the highest-severity finding in the whole scan: `severity 0.9`, `agent_risk 0.63`, 3.4× the 60-line domain-function threshold. It's also the dominant reason the file as a whole crossed the 300-line God File threshold (crime_00010). Extract cohesive sections (line-item building, tax/discount, totals, persistence/notify) into named helpers. Doing this once collapses *two* findings and gives every other refactor below a smaller surface to work against — so it goes first.\n\n## 2. Inject a clock (crime_00004)\nSeven direct `Date.now()` / `new Date()` calls at lines 57, 63, 92, 198, 238, 250, 273 make the billing pipeline non-deterministic and force any test to monkey-patch wall time. `test_gap 0.5` and a single weak-assertion test in `billing.test.ts` (crime_00006, crime_00016) say the safety net is already thin — every clock read in domain code makes it harder to add a real one. Thread a `now(): Date` (or `Clock`) through the call sites surfaced in step 1; the extraction makes the injection mechanical instead of invasive.\n\n## 3. Fix the refund seam: rename `calculateRefundPreview` *and* encode the comment rule (crime_00008 + crime_00011)\nThese two findings sit on adjacent lines (39 and 40–44) and describe the same hazard from two angles:\n- `calculateRefundPreview` reads as pure (`agent_risk 0.52`) but calls `saveRefundAudit` and `sendRefundEmail` — an agent will treat it as side-effect-free and call it speculatively.\n- The authorization rule *\"Only owners can refund enterprise plans unless billing support approves\"* lives in a comment, not in code (`agent_risk 0.5`).\n\nSplit into a pure `previewRefund(...)` and an explicit `executeRefund(...)`, and lift the owner/billing-support check into a named guard (e.g. `assertCanRefund(actor, plan)`) with a test. That converts the most agent-hostile spot in the file — a load-bearing prose rule guarded by a misleadingly-named function — into something a coding agent can actually see.\n\n---\n\nDeferred deliberately: the `\"enterprise\"` literal scatter (crime_00007) and the 4 TODOs (crime_00020) are real but lower-leverage; they're easier to handle once the function is decomposed and the refund seam is honest.",

import { execFile } from "node:child_process";
import { randomUUID } from "node:crypto";
import { existsSync, mkdirSync, readdirSync, readFileSync } from "node:fs";
import { writeFile, rename, mkdir, mkdtemp } from "node:fs/promises";
import { tmpdir } from "node:os";

import type { AgentRunResult } from "./agents/claude.js";
import { runJudge } from "./judge.js";
import { buildScanContext, runScan } from "./scan-helpers.js";
import { scoreStructural } from "./score.js";
import type {
  FixtureRegistryEntry,
  FixturesRegistry,
  Scenario,
  ScenarioKind,
  ScoreResult,
} from "./types.js";

const execFileAsync = promisify(execFile);

const HERE = dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url));
const REPO_ROOT = resolve(HERE, "..", "..", "..");
const FIXTURES_REGISTRY = resolve(
  REPO_ROOT,
  "evals",
  "fixtures",
  "fixtures.meta.json",
);
const SCENARIOS_DIR = resolve(REPO_ROOT, "evals", "scenarios");
const RESULTS_DIR = resolve(REPO_ROOT, "evals", "results");

const AGENTS = ["claude", "codex"] as const;
type Agent = (typeof AGENTS)[number];

interface CliFlags {
  agent?: Agent;
  fixture?: string;
  scenario?: ScenarioKind;
  judge: boolean;
  bail: boolean;
  concurrency: number;
  /**
   * Optional suffix appended to the version-keyed output directory.
   * Used for repeat-run variance sampling without burning a patch
   * version — e.g. `--label r2` writes to `evals/results/0.7.2-r2/`.
   */
  label?: string;
}

interface WorkItem {
  scenario: Scenario;
  fixture: FixtureRegistryEntry;
  agent: Agent;
}

interface Tally {
  total: number;
  passByAgent: Map<string, number>;
  totalByAgent: Map<string, number>;
  passByAgentKind: Map<string, number>;
  totalByAgentKind: Map<string, number>;
}

async function main(): Promise<void> {
  const startMs = Date.now();
  const flags = parseFlags(process.argv.slice(2));
  const ctx = await loadRunContext(flags);
  if (!ctx) return;

  const { fixturesToRun, scenariosToRun, usableAgents } = ctx;
  const items = buildWorkItems(fixturesToRun, scenariosToRun, usableAgents);
  if (items.length === 0) {
    process.stdout.write("evals: no scenarios match the supplied filters.\n");
    return;
  }

  const crimesVersion = await readCrimesVersion();
  const outDirName = flags.label
    ? `${crimesVersion}-${flags.label}`
    : crimesVersion;
  const outDir = resolve(RESULTS_DIR, outDirName);
  mkdirSync(outDir, { recursive: true });

  const scanCache = new Map<string, Promise<string>>();
  const tally = createTally();
  let completed = 0;

  await runPool(items, flags.concurrency, async (item) => {
    const seq = ++completed;
    process.stdout.write(
      `evals: [${seq}/${items.length}] ${item.agent} × ${item.scenario.id} (${item.fixture.name})\n`,
    );
    try {
      await processOne({ item, scanCache, flags, outDir, crimesVersion, tally });
    } catch (err) {
      const message = err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err);
      process.stderr.write(
        `evals: ${item.agent} × ${item.scenario.id} failed: ${message}\n`,
      );
      if (flags.bail) throw err;
    }
  });

  const summary = buildSummary(crimesVersion, scenariosToRun, usableAgents, tally);
  await writeJsonAtomic(resolve(outDir, "summary.json"), summary);
  const elapsed = formatDuration(Date.now() - startMs);
  process.stdout.write(
    `\nevals: done. ${tally.total} run × scenario combinations in ${elapsed}.\n` +
      `Results: ${outDir}\n`,
  );
}

interface RunContext {
  fixturesToRun: FixtureRegistryEntry[];
  scenariosToRun: Scenario[];
  usableAgents: Agent[];
}

async function loadRunContext(flags: CliFlags): Promise<RunContext | null> {
  if (!existsSync(FIXTURES_REGISTRY)) {
    process.stdout.write(
      `evals: ${FIXTURES_REGISTRY} not found — run \`pnpm run evals:setup\` first.\n`,
    );
    return null;
  }
  const registry = JSON.parse(
    readFileSync(FIXTURES_REGISTRY, "utf8"),
  ) as FixturesRegistry;
  const allScenarios = loadScenarios();
  if (registry.fixtures.length === 0 || allScenarios.length === 0) {
    process.stdout.write("evals: nothing to run (registry or scenarios empty).\n");
    return null;
  }

  const requestedAgents: Agent[] = flags.agent ? [flags.agent] : [...AGENTS];
  const usableAgents = await filterAvailableAgents(requestedAgents);
  if (usableAgents.length === 0) {
    process.stderr.write(
      "evals: no agent CLIs available. Install `claude` and/or `codex` and retry.\n",
    );
    process.exit(2);
    return null;
  }

  const fixturesToRun = registry.fixtures.filter(
    (f) => !flags.fixture || f.id === flags.fixture,
  );
  const scenariosToRun = allScenarios.filter((s) => {
    if (flags.scenario && s.kind !== flags.scenario) return false;
    return fixturesToRun.some((f) => f.id === s.fixture);
  });
  return { fixturesToRun, scenariosToRun, usableAgents };
}

async function filterAvailableAgents(requested: Agent[]): Promise<Agent[]> {
  const usable: Agent[] = [];
  for (const agent of requested) {
    if (await hasCommand(agent)) {
      usable.push(agent);
    } else {
      process.stderr.write(
        `evals: \`${agent}\` CLI not found on PATH — skipping ${agent} runs. ` +
          `Install it and re-authenticate, then re-run.\n`,
      );
    }
  }
  return usable;
}

function buildWorkItems(
  fixtures: FixtureRegistryEntry[],
  scenarios: Scenario[],
  agents: Agent[],
): WorkItem[] {
  const items: WorkItem[] = [];
  for (const scenario of scenarios) {
    const fixture = fixtures.find((f) => f.id === scenario.fixture);
    if (!fixture) continue;
    const fixtureDir = resolve(REPO_ROOT, fixture.path);
    if (!existsSync(fixtureDir)) {
      process.stderr.write(
        `evals: fixture ${fixture.path} not found on disk — skip (run evals:setup?).\n`,
      );
      continue;
    }
    for (const agent of agents) items.push({ scenario, fixture, agent });
  }
  return items;
}

interface ProcessOneArgs {
  item: WorkItem;
  scanCache: Map<string, Promise<string>>;
  flags: CliFlags;
  outDir: string;
  crimesVersion: string;
  tally: Tally;
}

async function processOne(args: ProcessOneArgs): Promise<void> {
  const { item, scanCache, flags, outDir, crimesVersion, tally } = args;
  const fixtureDir = resolve(REPO_ROOT, item.fixture.path);
  const scanJson = await getCachedScan(scanCache, fixtureDir);
  const scanContext = buildScanContext(scanJson);

  const agentResult = await invokeAgent(item.agent, item.scenario, scanJson);
  const structural = scoreStructural(
    agentResult.response,
    item.scenario.expected_artifacts,
    scanContext,
  );

  const result: ScoreResult = {
    scenario: item.scenario.id,
    agent: item.agent,
    crimes_version: crimesVersion,
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
    run_id: randomUUID(),
    response: agentResult.response,
    scan_context: scanContext,
    structural_score: structural,
  };

  if (flags.judge) {
    try {
      const judge = await runJudge({ scenario: item.scenario, response: agentResult.response });
      if (judge) result.judge_score = judge;
    } catch (err) {
      const message = err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err);
      process.stderr.write(
        `evals: judge pass failed for ${item.scenario.id} — ${message}\n`,
      );
    }
  }

The current local scan reports 79 findings, while the provided context reports 86, but the focused cluster in `src/index.ts`, `src/rollup.ts`, and `src/esbuild/svelte.ts` is the same shape.\\n\\n**Name Behavior Mismatch**\\n\\nFindings:\\n- `src/index.ts`: `build`, `buildAll`\\n- `src/rollup.ts`: `getRollupConfig`, `buildStart`\\n\\nRisk today:\\nAn agent may treat these as pure config/build-description helpers because names like `get*` and `build*` sound like calculation. They actually call side-effectful helpers such as `removeFiles`, `setSilent`, and logger setup. That makes it easy to move, memoize, reuse, or call them earlier without realizing they mutate global logging state or delete output files.\\n\\nRefactor plan:\\n- Rename orchestration functions to disclose effects:\\n  - `build` → `runBuild`\\n  - `buildAll` → `runBuildTasks` or `executeBuildEntries`\\n  - `getRollupConfig` → split into `createRollupOptions` plus `prepareRollupBuild`\\n  - Rollup plugin hook `buildStart` can stay as the Rollup-required hook name, but extract its deletion logic to `cleanRollupOutputBeforeBuild`.\\n- Pull destructive setup out of config constructors:\\n  - `cleanOutputFiles(...)`\\n  - `configureRollupLogging(...)`\\n  - `createRollupOutputOptions(...)`\\n- Keep pure config creation separately testable.\\n\\n**Option Bag Junk Drawer**\\n\\nFindings:\\n- `src/rollup.ts`: `getRollupConfig(options)` reads about 10 distinct fields.\\n- The supplied cluster mentions options in `src/index.ts`; the pasted scan specifically flags `normalizeOptions` as large and `build` as broad orchestration.\\n- Nearby related finding: `src/esbuild/index.ts:runEsbuild(options)` reads 38 fields, even though it is outside the requested three-file set.\\n\\nRisk today:\\nAn agent extending these modules has no crisp boundary between user config, normalized config, runtime-only state, and bundler-specific options. It could add another property to the generic bag, read a pre-normalized field after normalization, or accidentally pass CLI-only options into Rollup/esbuild internals.\\n\\nRefactor plan:\\n- Introduce explicit types for each phase:\\n  - `RawBuildOptions`\\n  - `NormalizedBuildOptions`\\n  - `RollupBuildOptions`\\n  - `SveltePluginOptions`\\n- In `src/index.ts`, make `normalizeOptions` return `NormalizedBuildOptions`, and only pass narrowed sub-objects downstream.\\n- In `src/rollup.ts`, change `getRollupConfig(options)` into something like:\\n  - `createRollupConfig(input: RollupConfigInput): RollupOptions`\\n  - `prepareRollupOutputs(input: RollupOutputInput): OutputOptions[]`\\n- Destructure at function boundaries so each helper declares ownership:\\n  ```ts\\n  function createRollupConfig({\\n    entry,\\n    format,\\n    external,\\n    outDir,\\n    outExtension,\\n  }: RollupConfigInput): RollupOptions\\n  ```\\n- Avoid passing the whole normalized app config into plugin/config factories.\\n\\n**Return Shape Roulette**\\n\\nFindings:\\n- `src/esbuild/svelte.ts`: `sveltePlugin`\\n- `src/esbuild/svelte.ts`: `setup`\\n- `src/esbuild/svelte.ts`: anonymous callback inside setup\\n\\nRisk today:\\nThe same function/callback returns object literals that represent different esbuild callback contracts: resolve results, load results, and error/warning payloads. Because there is no explicit return type, an agent may copy keys from one branch into another, return `{ code }` where esbuild expects `{ contents, loader }`, or mistake diagnostic objects like `{ text, location }` for plugin load results.\\n\\nRefactor plan:\\n- Add explicit esbuild callback return types:\\n  - `Plugin`\\n  - `OnResolveResult`\\n  - `OnLoadResult`\\n  - `PartialMessage` or the esbuild diagnostic type used by the installed version.\\n- Split callback bodies by contract:\\n  - `resolveSvelteImport(args): OnResolveResult`\\n  - `loadSvelteFile(args): Promise<OnLoadResult>`\\n  - `formatSvelteCompileError(error): PartialMessage`\\n  - `compileSvelteToJavaScript(source, filename): SvelteCompileResult`\\n- Name intermediate result variants instead of returning raw object literals from every branch.\\n- Keep `sveltePlugin(options): Plugin` as a thin adapter that wires esbuild hooks to those helpers.\\n\\n**Large Function / Large File**\\n\\nFindings:\\n- `src/index.ts`: `build`, anonymous build body, `mainTasks`, `normalizeOptions`, `buildAll`, `startWatcher`; file is also over the large-file threshold.\\n- `src/rollup.ts`: `getRollupConfig`\\n- `src/esbuild/svelte.ts`: `sveltePlugin`, `setup`, anonymous callback\\n\\nRisk today:\\nAn agent making a small behavior change has to keep too many invariants in working memory: cleaning, watch mode, entry normalization, DTS behavior, plugin setup, output config, and logging are interleaved.\\n\\nRefactor plan:\\n- `src/index.ts`\\n  - Extract `normalizeEntryOptions`, `normalizeDtsOptions`, `resolveOutDir`, `createBuildPlan`.\\n  - Split execution into `runOneTimeBuild(plan)` and `runWatchBuild(plan)`.\\n  - Keep exported `build(...)` as compatibility wrapper if public API stability matters, but make it delegate to `runBuild`.\\n- `src/rollup.ts`\\n  - Extract output creation, plugin list construction, declaration handling, and pre-build cleanup.\\n  - Keep Rollup hook objects small and move hook effects into named helpers.\\n- `src/esbuild/svelte.ts`\\n  - Keep `sveltePlugin` as declarative setup only.\\n  - Move resolve/load/compile/diagnostic behavior into typed helpers.\\n\\n**Order I’d Do It**\\n\\n1. Add named types first, without behavior changes.\\n2. Extract pure helpers from `src/esbuild/svelte.ts`, because explicit return types will catch contract drift quickly.\\n3. Split `src/rollup.ts` into pure config creation plus side-effect preparation.\\n4. Refactor `src/index.ts` last, preserving the public `build` export while moving internals to clearer `run*` / `create*` helpers.\\n5. Add focused tests around normalized options, Rollup config shape, and Svelte plugin resolve/load results before deleting old branches.\"}}\n{\"type\":\"turn.completed\",\"usage\":{\"input_tokens\":86923,\"cached_input_tokens\":59648,\"output_tokens\":1606,\"reasoning_output_tokens\":117}}\n",

/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.8.1/claude/context-04-monorepo.json:7:  "response": "## Agent context check: missing\n\n`crimes scan` flagged this repo with **crime_00009 — Missing Agent Context** (medium, confidence 0.9, agent_risk 0.61):\n\n- No `AGENTS.md` at repo root\n- No `CLAUDE.md` at repo root\n- No `.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md`\n- `package.json` declares bins (`tsup`, `tsup-node`) with no agent-discoverable docs\n\n## Consequence for agent work\n\nAn agent landing here has to infer everything from the file tree: that this is `tsup` (a bundler built on esbuild + rollup-for-dts), that `src/index.ts` is the orchestrator, that `src/utils.ts` is load-bearing (fan-in 18), and that several files are intentionally large (`src/index.ts:build` is 293 lines, `src/esbuild/index.ts:runEsbuild` is 237). Without that context, agents will:\n\n- Pick the wrong test runner / build command\n- Refactor God Functions that are large for non-obvious reasons\n- Miss that `src/plugin.ts` (fan-in 10) and `src/utils.ts` (fan-in 18) ripple widely\n- Re-add `Date.now()` calls that the team has already tolerated\n- Not know that `.test.ts` files use the local `test/utils.ts` harness (fan-in 9)\n\nThe eight High God Function findings and the High Fan-In files become guesswork without orientation.\n\n## Minimal AGENTS.md skeleton\n\n```markdown\n# AGENTS.md\n\n## What this is\ntsup — bundler powered by esbuild (JS/TS) and rollup (.d.ts).\nPublic entrypoint: `src/index.ts` (build orchestrator).\nCLI entrypoint: `src/cli-main.ts`.\n\n## Commands\n- Install: `pnpm install`\n- Build: `pnpm build`            # tsup builds itself\n- Test:  `pnpm test`             # vitest\n- Lint:  `pnpm lint`\n- Typecheck: `pnpm typecheck`\nRun the test you touched, not the full suite, when iterating.\n\n## Layout\n- `src/index.ts`         — main `build()` orchestrator; large by design\n- `src/cli-main.ts`      — argv → options → build\n- `src/esbuild/`         — esbuild plugins (postcss, svelte, swc, …)\n- `src/rollup.ts`        — rollup config builder for .d.ts\n- `src/api-extractor.ts` — Microsoft API Extractor integration\n- `src/plugin.ts`        — internal plugin contract (fan-in 10)\n- `src/utils.ts`         — shared helpers (fan-in 18, load-bearing)\n- `test/utils.ts`        — test harness used by every spec\n\n## Load-bearing files (read before editing)\n- `src/utils.ts`         — 18 importers\n- `src/plugin.ts`        — 10 importers, defines plugin lifecycle\n- `src/log.ts`           — 9 importers\n- `test/utils.ts`        — 9 importers (test contract)\n\n## Conventions\n- Plugins return esbuild/rollup-shaped objects; don't widen those return types.\n- `removeFiles`, `setSilent`, and `createLogger` have side effects despite\n  innocuous names — keep them in orchestrator code, not pure helpers.\n- Long functions in `src/index.ts`, `src/esbuild/index.ts`, and\n  `src/rollup.ts` are tolerated; prefer additive edits over splitting.\n\n## Don't\n- Don't introduce new sync I/O (`*Sync`) in domain code; async exists.\n- Don't add `Date.now()` / `new Date()` outside logging — bundle timings\n  are the only sanctioned use.\n- Don't restructure `src/utils.ts` or `src/plugin.ts` without checking\n  importers first.\n\n## Release\nChangesets-driven. See `CONTRIBUTING.md` for the release flow.\n```\n\nThat clears `crime_00009`: any one of `AGENTS.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, or a `SKILL.md` satisfies the detector. AGENTS.md is the most portable across coding agents.",

A button is reachable by Tab, activatable by Enter/Space, and announced as a button by screen readers — no `role`/`tabIndex`/`aria-label` workarounds needed. When the card is non-interactive (no `onActivate`), it stays a `<div>` with no click handler at all, which is the safer default than leaving a fake button behind. The suggested action in the finding (`switch_to_button_or_add_aria`) recommends exactly this swap; preferring the real element over `role=\\\\\\\"button\\\\\\\"` avoids re-implementing keyboard semantics by hand.\\\\n\\\\n2. **Design-token escape (hex literals in inline `style`)** — `#ff00aa` and `#0a0a0a` are inlined hex values that bypass the design system: they can't be themed, can't be audited for contrast, and won't track palette changes. They're replaced with Tailwind utility classes that resolve to named tokens (`bg-brand-accent`, `text-ink-900`). The exact token names depend on the project's `tailwind.config.*`; if those keys don't exist yet, add them there rather than re-inlining hex. Inline `style` is also dropped entirely, which removes the per-render style object allocation as a small bonus.\\\\n\\\\n3. **Responsive fragility (fixed `width: 800`)** — A hard-coded 800px width overflows any viewport under 800px and refuses to grow on larger ones. Option Bag Junk Drawer (`option_bag_junk_drawer`) — 1 finding in scope\n\n| Symbol | File | Bag | Fields read |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| `getRollupConfig` | src/rollup.ts | `options` | 10 distinct: `cjsInterop, clean, dts, entry, external, format, outDir, outExtension, …` |\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** Nothing in the signature tells the agent which fields are required, which are nested (`dts` is itself an object), which feed Rollup directly vs configure tsup's wrapper, or which combinations are mutually exclusive (`outExtension` interacts with `format` and `cjsInterop`). An agent extending Rollup output handling will guess — read `options.dts` as a boolean when it's `{ entry, resolve, … }`, or add a new field expecting it to be honored downstream when nothing reads it.\n\n**Refactor.** Define a `NormalizedRollupOptions` interface (or zod schema if it's exposed at a boundary) listing exactly the 10 fields the function consumes, with their nested shapes. Destructure at the top of the function:\n\n```ts\nconst { cjsInterop, clean, dts, entry, external, format, outDir, outExtension, … } = options;\n```\n\nThat single change moves the implicit contract into the type system and gives the IDE/agent a discoverable answer to \"what does this function actually need?\"\n\n---\n\n## 3. Return Shape Roulette (`return_shape_roulette`) — 3 findings, all in src/esbuild/svelte.ts\n\n| Symbol | Lines | Shapes | Example divergence |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| `sveltePlugin` | 9–124 | 6 | `{ args, namespace, path }` vs `{ location, text }` |\n| `setup` | 17–122 | 6 | same |\n| inner anonymous | 31–121 | 5 | `{ location, text }` vs `{ code }` |\n\n**What's actually going on.** These aren't one function returning six shapes — they're a stack of esbuild plugin callbacks (`onResolve`, `onLoad`, error reporters) nested inside `setup`, each with a *different* esbuild-defined return contract. The detector reads them as one big arrow because they're inline.\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** An agent asked to \"fix the svelte plugin to support a new file extension\" sees one 91-line arrow and assumes the return shape is fungible. It returns `{ contents, loader }` from what is actually an `onResolve` branch (which wants `{ path, namespace }`), and esbuild silently routes the wrong way — the build fails far from the edit, or worse, succeeds with wrong output.\n\n**Refactor.** Two moves, in order:\n\n1. **Annotate.** Import esbuild's plugin types and annotate each callback: `build.onResolve(filter, (args): OnResolveResult => { … })`, `build.onLoad(filter, (args): OnLoadResult => { … })`. The compiler will immediately reject cross-contract returns.\n2. **Extract each callback to a named top-level helper** (`resolveSvelteImport`, `loadSvelteFile`, `formatCompileError`) with explicit return types. Pass the esbuild `PluginBuild` in. The plugin factory becomes ~15 lines that wire callbacks to filters — readable at a glance.\n\n---\n\n## Cross-cutting note\n\nAll five findings share one root cause: **types and names absorb work that comments and convention currently do**. The fix isn't extraction for its own sake — it's making the type system describe contracts an agent currently has to infer from 100+ line bodies. Doing the False Identity renames first is cheapest; the option bag and return-shape work pays off most.",

/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.8/claude/refactor-01-petty.json:7:  "response": "## Three petty crimes in `src/petty.ts`\n\n### 1. `describeCheckout` — Option Bag Junk Drawer (crime_00022, L10–19)\n\n**Finding type:** `option_bag_junk_drawer`. The function takes a single `options` object and reaches into 6 unrelated fields (`plan`, `role`, `region`, `currency`, `status`, `retry`). The named `CheckoutOptions` interface is technically there, but the function still treats the bag as a junk drawer — every field is required, every field is read once, and the bag isn't shaped around what the function *does* (build a colon-joined fingerprint).\n\n**Agent risk today:** an agent extending this — say, adding `tier` to the fingerprint — will add an optional `tier?: string` to `CheckoutOptions` because that's the path of least resistance, and silently broaden the contract for every other caller that constructs a `CheckoutOptions`. There's no signal that this function owns only the fingerprint, not the canonical checkout shape.\n\n**Refactor:** destructure the fields the function owns, and name the result.\n\n```ts\nexport function describeCheckout({\n  plan, role, region, currency, status, retry,\n}: CheckoutOptions): string {\n  return [plan, role, region, currency, status, String(retry)].join(\":\");\n}\n```\n\nIf `CheckoutOptions` is shared, give the function its own narrower input type (`CheckoutFingerprintInput`) so adding fields to the global checkout shape doesn't silently change the fingerprint.\n\n---\n\n### 2. `resolvePaymentResult` — Return Shape Roulette (crime_00023, L21–32)\n\n**Finding type:** `return_shape_roulette`. Three branches, three disjoint return shapes, **0% key overlap** between `{ ok, code, message }`, `{ url, permanent }`, and `{ ok, value, cached }`. No declared return type, so callers (and TypeScript inference) see a wide union with every field optional.\n\n**Agent risk today:** an agent writing `if (result.ok) … else useUrl(result.url)` will get green types and a runtime `undefined` — the `ok: false` branch carries `code/message`, not `url`. The `redirect` branch doesn't even set `ok`, so `if (!result.ok)` matches both the error case *and* the redirect case. An agent extending this with a fourth branch has no template to follow.\n\n**Refactor:** make the shape a discriminated union and declare it.\n\n```ts\ntype PaymentResult =\n  | { kind: \"error\"; code?: string; message?: string }\n  | { kind: \"redirect\"; url?: string; permanent: boolean }\n  | { kind: \"ok\"; value?: string; cached: boolean };\n\nexport function resolvePaymentResult(input: {…}): PaymentResult {\n  if (input.error)    return { kind: \"error\",    code: input.code, message: input.message };\n  if (input.redirect) return { kind: \"redirect\", url: input.url, permanent: false };\n  return                       { kind: \"ok\",     value: input.value, cached: false };\n}\n```\n\nNow callers switch on `result.kind` and the compiler narrows fields correctly.\n\n---\n\n### 3. `canRunBilling` — Negative Flag Maze (crime_00024, L34–37)\n\n**Finding type:** `negative_flag_maze`. Two negative flags (`disableBilling`, `skipRetry`) combined with `!`. The body reduces to \"return whether both negatives are false,\" which is `!a && !b` — readable today, a coin flip after one edit.\n\n**Agent risk today:** the moment a third condition arrives, an agent has to mentally invert each flag *and* preserve the conjunction. A common slip is \"if billing is enabled OR retry is allowed\" → `!disableBilling || !skipRetry`, which is the wrong predicate. Negative flag names also invite double-negative call sites (`canRunBilling(!enableBilling, !retryAllowed)`).\n\n**Refactor:** flip the flag polarity at the API boundary and name the predicate.\n\n```ts\nexport function canRunBilling({\n  billingEnabled,\n  retryAllowed,\n}: { billingEnabled: boolean; retryAllowed: boolean }): boolean {\n  return billingEnabled && retryAllowed;\n}\n```\n\nCallers that still hold the negative flags pass `billingEnabled: !disableBilling, retryAllowed: !skipRetry` at the call site — one inversion, in one place, instead of every reader doing it in their head.\n\n---\n\n**Common thread:** all three are low-severity, high-agent-risk patterns — they don't bite humans because we read the whole tiny file in one go, but they bite agents because each function's contract is implicit. Naming the shape (input bag, return union, flag polarity) is the cheapest possible fix and turns each function into something the next agent can extend without re-reading the caller list.",
